sentences.text: Yeah.
As a woman?
Keep the kosher home.
Make your husband-- make meals, take-- raise-- raise the children, family.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Do some-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it had to-- had to do with money.
Money, I can use it as much as you-- as I can think is advisable to spend.
Not to spend more than you needed.
And take care on the house for only house.
Help your husband in business, if he is in business.
Be a partner, 50-50, with your husband.
That's all.
That's all that's a woman's work is.
Raise an honest family.
A good family should be-- raise children.
They should grow up nice, good citizens, wherever country you live in.
What about community life?
We used to come together, girls.
We had some singing, and playing, drawing, knitting, sewing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Spare time in the evenings, you come in, you sing, you knit, learn how to needle, crocheting.
That's just what you did.
And go to school.
In spare time.
Vacation time, you  do another things.
And school-- most important was schooling.
You have to get knowledge.
Even as a woman, you  have to be knowledgeable.
Not-- Get knowledge.
It's very important.
Votings, understand, read papers and books.
My-- I had an uncle, uncle was a librarian.
Used to go to the library, and get either the teacher and the librarian, both things.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So get books, read all kind of books.
Russian books.
Jewish books.
Polish books.
And the Russian came in, they-- they [? burned the-- ?] they-- confiscated all the library, because they didn't like this-- all the books.
It's kapitalistisch books, they said, no.
And my father-- my [? grandma-- ?] my uncle was without a job, then.
He became a-- he was a bookkeeper, was working in an office in bookkeepers.
My aunt was a devoted woman, too.
Very devoted.
Used to invite me every-- Come in, come in.
Used to make gefilte fish, used to treat me with fish, with sweet rolls.
She liked me very much.
This was my father's sister.
Jewish, very Orthodox woman, too.
Very charitable woman.

She used to go around and Saturday used to go around between the Jewish houses and collect pieces of challah, whole basket with challah, and used to bring it in to the Jewish old home, to feed the old people.
But it's challah, and sometimes chicken soup, somebody'd put some extra on.
Poor people, for poor old people, bring it in to the Jewish old home.
The Jewish old home.
They called us [SPEAKING IN HEBREW], it's a Hebrew-- a Hebrew name.
Used to go there.
She was with another woman.
Two woman.
Very nice.
That's the way I was raised, in this kind of my environment.
To give charity.
To help poor people.
To help sick people.
To help everybody what is in need.
Before the war?
I was in-- In school, I didn't feel anti-Semitism.
Because the school was all Jewish. 73% were Jews there.
So all the children are all Jewish.
But outside, there was a few Polish stores.
They used to sell merchandise to-- to the Polish people, the-- you know, the-- so they used to write on signs, 'Don't buy from a Jew.' Don't-- [SPEAKING IN POLISH] This was in Polish.
They used to made up some pictures from Jews with long noses.
That was my first experience.
I never had these things.
The look, the face with a long nose, with a beard, that big kapote, you know, the dress.
That was after Pilsudski, the-- the-- the-- he was the head of Polish-- Polish Poland, he died.
That time start, the start anti-Semitism, very bad.
The Germans was working there already.
That-- That time I feel it already it's very bad.
And my uncle was very knowledgeable.
Used to say it's-- it's a bad time.
Very bad time.
He studied the paper, it's not good for Jews to stay here.
I was trying to-- I was too young-- trying to run away from, go away.
But was no way to go away from Poland.
I was still too young.
I couldn't go on a kibbutz.
When you're 15 years old, they don't take you.
They take you when you're 18. 14, 15, 16, they don't take you.
So I was waiting till I get 18.
I could go run away from Poland.
Then was the war. 1939, September, came the war.
And Poland was divide-- divide in two.
In two.
One part is-- was Russian, to the middle.
And another part was German.
They made up-- they made a limit, and they divided Poland.
So that night, when they-- when the Russian came in, was flying that airplanes, and tanks, and lot of-- lot of soldiers was noisy.
But they didn't do nothing to us.
They just came in, and they-- they-- cleaned up all the stores.
Wherever somebody had stores, they cleaned up inside the stores.
But, you know, they-- they-- they give you-- they paid you with money, but the money wasn't worth nothing.
And they established the elder Jewish, in the Jewish institution was closed up.
It was a Jewish school, the shul was closed up.
Everything was closed up.
And they start to put up their-- their-- their style of life.
The-- But then the people from Russia, they put up-- the head of the-- of the town was like the-- the-- was a Russian guy.
He-- He installed the rules from Russia.
And just the working people to do something with it.
And the more the businesspeople was not too  good for the businesspeople.
They started to-- They didn't give you good work.
You couldn't go to school.
Part of because your-- your father was a businessman, the put up in the passport that your father's a businessman, it's not good.
If they put  it up as a little businessman, it was a little bit better.
But if he is a big businessman, they wouldn't let you out from the city.
But fortunately, my father was written.
He is a good man, so they put it up little businessman.
So I had opportunity to go to a different city.
Get schooling, get education, more education, like the Rowne, the biggest city.
And then, therefore, 19-- the end of 19-- 1940-- '39, I went to another, like in January, went to another school there.
It was getting-- They pay me for schooling.
In Russia, they pay for schooling.
They give you board, room and board, and they pay extra-- extra roubles in packet, and you should go to school.
So I went there.
I was in school there from January '39, '40, till the war, '41, I was in school there.
The big town.
Was a big experience for me, because I never went out from my town.
I met some different girls from different towns.
And boys, you know.
And used to have-- but we had-- you had to-- you have to learn very hard.
It's very hard, because we had new languages.
From Polish you have to go on Russian, and in Ukrainian.
It's-- It's very hard, but we strived.
I managed it.
I made it.
And then the war came between Russian-- Russia and-- and Poland-- I mean, Russia and Germany.
That was a bad experience that time.
They bombed the city.
I had to run away.
My father-- when I came back home, there was worrying about me.
I came back in a military car.
And-- And the 70 kilometers was so far away, that time, because the aeroplanes from the Germans was on [INAUDIBLE] bombing.
And-- And-- And very dangerous.
A lot of people perished that time.
And I finally came back home.
So my-- my father was so happy to see me, and mother.
They thought I'm dead already.
And I was-- was waiting till the German came in.
The German came in.
I want to go away deep in Russia, but my father didn't let us.
You are girls.
It's very dangerous to go for you.
And you're young.
So you stay here.
Whatever it's gonna be with us, it will with you.
So that's why I left-- was left with the Germans.
Waiting for the Germans, and the German came in.
Started to German, and the German came in.
So right away, and we saw already that some terrible things happened to us.
Already was starting to grab us to work.
They didn't even give us food.
They give us only 10 out of this 20 grams of bread a day.
That was a very little piece of grain.
If only 20 grams is like a-- a fifth of a kilogram. 100 grams is a kilogram.
And 20 grams is the like dark bread, you know.
The give you-- You stayed in the-- in the line, and you have to get the bread.
But I used to go out in the market to buy something by-- by the farmers.
One time I went out in the market.
A German-- by [INAUDIBLE] was like a highway to town.
Used to pass by from Lvov-- Lvov to Kiev.
Was a highway.
So I used to come.
The-- The German soldiers used to ride the highway going to the front.
So some SS people used to pass by.
And so I had to wear a-- a patch.
In front, and in the back.
A yellow patch.
Because I was a Jew.
But I used to run out.
My mother and father was home.
We used to-- I used to run-- [? alone, ?] I used to run out to buy something a little-- a little fresh potatoes or something in vegetables.
They used to bring it up.
One hour you had time to buy something.
One German was saw-- saw me with the two patches.
He run down from his machine, you know, from his car.
And he run with a big stick.
And I didn't know it, the first time I experience it.
And he runs after me, and I see he's running, so I run away.
He was running faster than I am.
He was beating me in my shoulders, in my head, all over he beat me up.
I came in home, I was black and blue from that experience.
That was the first experience, Germans beat me up.
I'm buying some food in the market.
Terrible.
Was very upset, very demoralized.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That I'm not a human being.
How come he beats me up?
I couldn't understand.
For what?
Because I am a Jew.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That was a very bad experience.
From that time on, I understand it.
I have no right.
They can do anything to me.
And nobody will say something.
From the output I was stuck.
Then they start dragging us to work.
I was working in-- on the-- and the houses were demolished.
A lot of houses on the freeway were demolished.
So we had to pick up all the pieces, a lot of girls.
They mobilized the girls to work on the thing, to clean up the houses and clean up the bricks.
The bricks were still not clean.
You have to clean them up and put them together on stacks.
Was working all-- all the time with that work.
Winter in '42, I got another job.
The winter time, they give you another.
The summertime, we were do on the thing, cleaning up the thing.
And then in winter time, they give us a job to work on a-- to-- to sort potatoes in a cellar, down in the basement.
Dark.
So about 40, 50 girls, and a couple men, was working there.
And I was put between them.
And a couple Germans was watching us.
To get up early in the morning, 6:00 and come back night-- at night-- late at night.
The benefit from that work was, I-- we stole potatoes.
There we did it, that we stole the potatoes.
We stole the potatoes in the basement.
Inside.
Clean them up, and put them in a big pot, and put them in the inside.
And I was afraid maybe they're gonna catch us.
They can kill us for that.
But hunger is worse than killing, was hungry.
Brought in potatoes, and then cooked the potatoes.
There was no fire in the house.
The only fire we put up is to cook a potato.
Cold.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The winter from '41, '42, was the worst winter I had in my life-- in my lifetime.
Cold, hungry, undernourished.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had my mother's mother came in.
Was staying with us.
The older person, 82 years old.
The only thing we could give her to eat is a piece of potato.
That's all.
And that-- that winter, she died from hunger.
Winter until summertime.
Then was springtime.
Springtime was the big, '42, was the big roundup, the killing.
And my grandmother died Pesach, that was April, before the killing.
That was the working.
Whether you work [INAUDIBLE] before the roundup was, most of the time, the Ukrainian people helped the people, the Nationalist.
They-- they want to have their own country, you know, separate-- a separate Ukraine, not to belong to the Russia, to somebody else.
So these people came out, and they are, was ruling.
There was not too many of them.
Just the village surround.
These people came out, and they did terrible things to us.
Terrible things the did to us.
They-- they find out all the Jews, where they are.
They-- they rounded up Jews, helped to round up Jews.
Whatever the Germans told them.
They helped.
They helped the German government to do it.
Because they didn't, the German didn't know who was a Jew, who's not a Jew.
They told them.
The Ukrainian people.
The teacher, what was teaching us at school, [INAUDIBLE], he was the-- he was a little bit in the government there, too, but later on didn't agree with them, they-- he backed up.
But another ones, would-- people where they collaborated with the Germans.
Especially the-- the-- name-- they called him [INAUDIBLE].
There was their ruler was in-- near Lvov, Ukrainian.
This was the West Ukrainian.
The ruler would give them instructions, and they should fight for their own land.
To be-- to be a land for themself, not to belong to nobody.
So these-- these people give us a lot of trouble.
Some of them was nice.
Not-- not a lot of them.
Very few.
Very few.
In fact, I was saved by the people.
But not the West-- the West Ukrainian.
The East Ukrainian.
My mother was born there.
And she knew all the people.
And that's why we went there, after the second-- the liquidation of the ghetto, we had only one choice to go.
Over there.
My mother said, the only chance I can save you was going to my village.
Maybe.
She didn't promise us for sure.
Maybe, she said, we can save ourself.
We'll see.
But try to go.
They round up the great-- the-- the first roundup we-- we stayed alive, because we hide ourself on attic.
And that thanks to my sister.
My-- In the morning, we heard some shots around the town.
So my sister, when I waked up too, my father, my mother, all of us waked up.
It was still dark, early in the morning in May, it was dark still.
So my father-- mother opened the window and talked to our neighbor, said what's going on?
She says, then another neighbor said, it looks like they are rounding us up.
Everything is rounded up, the Gestapo and-- and Ukrainian police.
Do-- Do-- Do what you can, because I think they 're taking everybody now.
So my mother said, we don't have no choice were to go now.
We have to go on the attic.
Next building from us was a-- like a-- from the war, on the bombs was demolished half a house.
So the attic was still OK, so my mother said-- and our attic was separated from their attic.
My mother said, let's-- I think we'll have to go on that attic.
But here, they're gonna look for us.
Over there was demolished.
Nobody will look, maybe, for us.
So we'll go there on the attic.
We went up on the attic.
All the family.
My mother, my father, myself, and my sister.
That was still dark.
But the door, the entrance door, we didn't open up.
We left it like locked.
Like-- you know, to night, to bed.
Locked up.
My sister says, oy!
You didn't lock-- you didn't open the door?
They're going to look for us.
She went down, opened the door, and the minute she went upstairs, the Germans was already in the house.
And she saved our lives that time.
I was sitting on the attic.
So all the things we'd been through, they was, all the Jews was going through our street-- children, women, old people, marching quiet, didn't hear a word from them.
Nothing.
Everything was so quiet, in both side.
And we looked through the little holes, you known, from the attic.
The little-- And we saw everything.
We see it.
My sister was so nervous, and shaking herself so badly, my mother couldn't-- couldn't stand.
There was something like hiding there on the attic, so she put her on to the-- like a bench, like a little-- She put her onto that bench.
She-- She was shivering like a-- like a leaf from-- from-- from afraid, she was afraid.
She was very afraid.
Scared.
Shivering like a leaf.
Until 4 o'clock. 4 o'clock we was sitting there, and my father was a smoker.
Big smoker.
He used to smoke all week.
But Saturday-- Friday evening, no smoke till Saturday evening at night.
He used to cough a little bit.
My father.
But that time, in the attic, he didn't cough.
Nothing.
Quiet.
Everybody was quiet.
It was late, and my sister was quiet down.
My mother quiet her down.
She was a kid.
A little kid still.
To quiet her down a little bit, she was to quiet down already.
By 4 o'clock, we hear some voices from people.
And they said, you can go out, wherever you're hiding places.
You can go out, because it's now, they are-- they are making a ghetto, and you have to go.
Everybody has to go in the ghetto.
That was the day from the first, you know, gathering people.
That's the way we hide.
We stayed alive.
According to my mother and my sister.
That was May 21st.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then we went out.
We came down.
In fact, somebody was, the Ukrainian people was open.
They used to go-- they went in all-- all the houses.
They rape, they take all the things away from the houses.
My father saw they was taking some things from the house.
He said to my mother, I want to go down.
I know the guy.
You shouldn't take my-- my things from the-- My mother, keep quiet!
They're going to discover us.
Let them take whatever they wanted.
So the windows, they drag out all the things.
Everything.
The people, they-- they killed the people, and they were having a ball.
They had a ball.
They had some full of baskets.
They couldn't carry.
They carry them, so they used to take up, rip apart the-- the pillowcases with feathers.
And they used the pillowcases and stuffed them with goods.
With Jewish goods and trinkets.
You watched this?
I watched this.
I saw this.
They left-- they left only the feathers in the house.
And they didn't have so many things to put in.
They didn't have some basket.
The have nothing.
They're poor.
But then they want to take it at once.
So they took-- they let out all the-- all the feathers, and they put it in inside everything.
And they grabbed everything what we have in the house.
Everything.
In fact, after the war, I went in the market, and I find a coat what they-- what they took away from my house.
My coat.
And I remember-- And I-- And I recognized my coat.
I ran into that woman.
And I told her, I want to buy the coat from you.
Stay in the same place.
She was staying there, she wants to sell it.
She asked me some money.
And I went back to the police, and I said, there is a woman what she's-- what she's selling my coat, which they took away from my house.
Come on with me.
He came back.
He came with me.
And I said-- And he said to her, this is not your goods.
Give it up to-- the coat belongs to this girl.
Give her back the coat.
She was so scared, and shaking.
She gave me the coat.
I says, let her take-- I want to take her to the police station.
She has some more stuff of mine, but I want to.
So was a lot of people.
It was a market day.
A lot of people.
And he was crying to the-- to the policeman.
So she turned around with the people, and she disappeared.
The only thing what there was left is with the coat.
My husband is still mad at me.
I should take her to the police, he says.
Why didn't you take her to the police?
I couldn't take her.
Can I grab her?
That was the experience from the Ukrainian, what they did to the  all Jewish people.
They-- they took everything from us away.
Let's cut for the moment.
Yeah.
[INAUDIBLE].
